blocks.content: It wasn't long. It wasn't nothing. It was a real thing that happened, on the catwalk of the water tower on a flat grey November Saturday, and he let it happen, and somewhere in the middle of it he looked past her shoulder at the view and his eyes went without deciding to — straight to the field.

She was smiling. It was a good smile. She was a good person — he knew that, had always known that — and that was somehow the part that made it worse, the fact that she'd done nothing wrong, that there was nothing here to be angry at except himself.

She looked at him for a second with the directness she always had, the thing about her that never hedged, and then she turned back to the view and he looked at the field below and the November sky above it and the specific shape of the houses on Magnolia Street visible from up here if you knew where to look.
They climbed down without saying much. On the walk back, Brooke slipped her hand into his and he let her, and he thought: this is fine. This is a normal thing. This is what people do.
He thought it all the way home.
